coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanocrystals decorated AgVO3 nanowires with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xue
Xu, Da
Jiang, Shanshan
Xie, Fei
Song, Minshan
Zhai, Hongju
Zhao, Lina
Che, Guangbo
Chang, Limin
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a novel graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanocrystals/AgVO3 photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a simple two-step method. The as-prepared g-C3N4/AgVO3 heterostructures included g-C3N4 nanocrystals assembling uniformly on the surface of AgVO3 nanowires. The successful modification of AgVO3 nanowires by g-C3N4 nanocrystals was found to improve the photocatalyst activity significantly because of enhanced light absorption and improved separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanocrystals
AgVO3
Heterostructure
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of as-synthesized g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 (a), and HRTEM images of g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 (b, c).

(a) Photodegradation efficiencies of Rh B as a function of irradiation time for different photocatalysts. (b) Room temperature PL spectra of as-synthesized samples under the excitation wavelength of 325nm. (c) Cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B over g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 sample under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanocrystals decorated AgVO3 nanowires with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Applications of Environmental Friendly Materials (Jilin Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun 130103, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Applications of Environmental Friendly Materials (Jilin Normal University)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematics and Physics, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a novel graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanocrystals/AgVO3 photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a simple two-step method. The as-prepared g-C3N4/AgVO3 heterostructures included g-C3N4 nanocrystals assembling uniformly on the surface of AgVO3 nanowires. The successful modification of AgVO3 nanowires by g-C3N4 nanocrystals was found to improve the photocatalyst activity significantly because of enhanced light absorption and improved separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of visible-light-driven photocatalysts with excellent performance and good stability is a prerequisite for harvesting more sunlight and realizing efficient photocatalysis [1,2]. AgVO3 has been demonstrated to be an efficient visible photocatalyst, due to its narrow band gap and well crystallization [3]. However, poor quantum yield and poor visible light absorption efficiency are still challenges to improve the photocatalytic performance of AgVO3 for meeting the practical application requirements. To solve this issue, great effort has been devoted to the exploration and preparation of novel photocatalytic materials for improving photocatalytic performance of the catalysts [3–5], including dye-sensitization [5–7], development of new narrow-band gap semiconductors and band engineering of semiconductor heterojunction [8–12]. Among these works, the construction of a semiconductor heterojunction has attracted great attention because of its perfect effectiveness in improving photocatalytic activity [13–17].

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a metal-free polymeric semiconductor has attracted much attention [18]. In this context, it would be of great significance to develop g-C3N4 based materials for photocatalysis application. Recently, there are a few reports concentrated on g-C3N4 based photocatalysts, such as g-C3N4 quantum dots/g-C3N4, g-C3N4/graphene, g-C3N4/BiPO4, and g-C3N4/TiO2 [19–22]. Herein, we report a successful attempt at the fabrication of g-C3N4/AgVO3 nanowires via a facile in situ precipitation method, and the photocatalytic activity of the nanoheterostructures was investigated by measuring the degradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) under visible light (λ >420nm).

Bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by heating melamine for 4h to 550°C and kept at this temperature for another 4h in air [19]. The g-C3N4 nanocrystals were prepared by using the Yu method [19]: First, 1g of bulk g-C3N4 was treated in the mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (20mL) and nitric acid (HNO3) (20mL) for about 2h at room temperature. The mixture was then diluted with deionized water (1L) and washed for several times. Second, 50mg of the obtained solid was dispersed in 30mL concentrated NH3·H2O, and then the mixed suspension was transferred into a 20mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 24h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was washed with water for several times to remove the adsorbed NH3 molecules. Third, 10mg of the synthesized solid was dispersed in 100mL water, and then ultrasound for about 6h. The as-obtained aqueous suspension was then centrifuged at about 8000rpm, and dialyzed in a dialysis bag to remove large-sized nanoparticles.

AgVO3 nanowires were prepared based on a facile hydrothermal method. For more details about the preparation of the AgVO3 nanowires, the readers are referred to our reported work [23]. The as-prepared AgVO3 nanowires (300mg) were mixed with 200mL of deionized water by ultrasonication for 30min. Then, 1.0mL of 5% polyethylene glycol (PEG) 2000 solution was added and the dispersion was stirred for another 10min. To get a well-dispersed g-C3N4 aqueous solution, g-C3N4 was protonated with concentrated HCl solution [24]. 0.5g of the g-C3N4 were treated by a post-processing with HCl (18.5wt%, 25mL) for 4h of protonation at room temperature. Before being filtered, the g-C3N4/HCl/H2O suspension was diluted with 200mL of deionized water. Then the protonated g-C3N4 was washed with deionized water until neutral conditions and dried at 70°C in air for 4h. 50mg of the as-prepared protonated g-C3N4 was ultrasonically dispersed in deionized water for 6h to get well-dispersed aqueous solution of 1mgmL−1. For wrapping g-C3N4 on AgVO3 nanowires, a certain amount (3, 6 and 12mL) of the g-C3N4 solution (1mgmL−1) was added and the reaction temperature was kept at 70°C for 60min. The resulting suspension was filtered, washed with deionized water three times and dried at 60°C for 24h in a vacuum oven. The theoretical wrapping amount of g-C3N4 was 1, 2, and 4wt%.

The crystal structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku (Japan) D/max 2500 X-ray diffractometer (Cu K α radiation, λ =0.154 18nm). The morphologies and structure details of the as-synthesized samples were detected by using field emission scanning microscopy (SEM, JSM-6510) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalysts were obtained by a F4500 (Hitachi, Japan) photoluminescence detector with an excitation wavelength of 325nm. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded using a scan UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550).

The photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared samples were evaluated using Rh B as a model compound. In experiments, the Rh B solution (0.01mmol·L−1, 100mL) containing 0.05g of photocatalyst was mixed in a Pyrex reaction glass. A 300W Xe lamp (λ >420nm) was employed to provide visible light irradiation. A 420nm cut-off filter was inserted between the lamp and the sample to filter out UV light (λ <420nm). Prior to visible light illumination, the suspension was strongly stirred in the dark for 40min. Then the solution was exposed to visible light irradiation under magnetic stirring. At given time intervals, 4mL of the suspension was periodically collected and analyzed after centrifugation. The Rh B concentration was analyzed by a UV-2550 spectrometer to record intensity of the maximum band at 552nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared AgVO3, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 samples. The diffraction peaks for the AgVO3 sample are well indexed to monoclinic structure of β-AgVO3 (JCPDS: 29-1154) [3]. When coupling the two compounds, the main characteristic diffraction peaks of AgVO3 did not change obviously. In addition, there is no any diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 can be detected for g-C3N4/AgVO3 sample, which may be resulted from small crystal size or low percentage of g-C3N4 nanocrystals.

Herein, the successful loading of g-C3N4 was illustrated by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of the as-synthesized AgVO3, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 samples are displayed in Fig. S1. In the spectrum of g-C3N4, several absorption bands at 1700–800cm−1 are ascribed to either CN or CN stretching vibrations [24]. The broad adsorption band centered at 3200cm−1 is attributed to the NH stretching vibration [24]. The main characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 are detected in the spectrum of g-C3N4/AgVO3 sample, showing the successful loading of g-C3N4.

XPS survey spectra of the as-obtained g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 are shown in Fig. S2. To identify different contributions of the C 1s (Fig. S2a) and N 1s (Fig. S2b) peaks, the spectrum was deconvoluted into various peaks as shown. From the results, the C 1s peak at 283.9eV is assigned to the CC bond in the turbostratic CN structure and the peak at 287.8eV is attached to the sp3 CN bond of the sp3 bonded composition [23]. The N 1s peaks are comprised of two components centered at 397.5eV and 400.7eV, which are identified as the CNC and (N(C)3) groups, respectively [23]. As illustrated in Fig. S2c, typical peaks of Ag 3d can be observed, in which the peaks at 366.5 and 372.5eV are ascribed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively [23]. The corresponding peaks are symmetric, which can mainly be attributed to the Ag+ ions in AgVO3. XPS signals of V 2p are observed at binding energy at about 515.5eV (Fig. S2d), ascribed to V5+ [23]. Compared with pure AgVO3 nanowires, slight shift in the binding energy of V5+ can be observed (Fig. S2e), due to the interfacial interactions between the phases of g-C3N4 and AgVO3.

The SEM images of the as-prepared AgVO3 and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 samples are shown in Fig. S3. It can be seen that the pure AgVO3 sample was composed of nanowires with widths of around 50nm, and lengths of up to several micrometers (Fig. S3a). The g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 sample shows a similar morphology to pure AgVO3 (Fig. S3b), revealing that low amount g-C3N4 nanocrystals loading did not have any great influence on the morphology of AgVO3 nanowires. In order to further ascertain the nanojunction of g-C3N4 and AgVO3, AgVO3 and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 samples were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), as displayed in Fig. S3c and Fig. 2 . Fig. S3c shows the threadlike structure of the as-synthesized AgVO3. In addition, several gray particles are also observed in the image of AgVO3, with the diameter being 10–50nm, which may be due to incomplete crystallization of these particles. It is clearly shown in Fig. 2a that the g-C3N4 nanocrystals with diameters of around 5nm were loaded on the surface of AgVO3 nanowire. To obtain a well-dispersed g-C3N4 aqueous solution, g-C3N4 was protonated with concentrated HCl solution [24]. It can be detected in Fig. 2a that the protonated g-C3N4 with a breaking up of stacks and the small sizes would be in favor of g-C3N4 wrapping on the surface of AgVO3 nanowires. The purpose of the addition of PEG 2000 aqueous solution is to introduce the hydroxyl groups to the surface of AgVO3 nanowires [24]. Fig. 2b and c show the HRTEM images of the as-prepared g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 nanowires. By measuring the lattice fringes, the resolved interplanar distances are about 0.35 and 0.336nm, which corresponds to the (110) plane of AgVO3, and the (002) plane of g-C3N4, respectively.

Fig. S4a shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of as-fabricated AgVO3, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 samples. The as-synthesized samples exhibited an intense absorption in the visible-light range, which reveals the property of being photoactive under visible-light illumination. The absorbance edge of g-C3N4/AgVO3 composite showed marked red shifts, which therefore improved the utilization of the solar spectrum. The band gap energies of the pure g-C3N4 and AgVO3 can be calculated by the following formula:
(1)
αhv = A
hv −
E g
n / 2
where α, v, E g and A are absorption coefficient, light frequency, the band-gap energy, and a constant, respectively. Among these parameters, n is determined by the type of optical transition of a semiconductor (n =1 for a direct transition and n =4 for an indirect transition). For g-C3N4 and AgVO3, the value of n is 1 for the direct transition [13,23]. According to Eq. (1), the band-gap energies (E
g ) of g-C3N4 and AgVO3 can be estimated from a plot of (ahv)2 versus energy (hv), as revealed in Fig. S4b. Thus, the band gaps of the as-prepared g-C3N4 and AgVO3 are calculated to be 2.73eV and 2.00eV, respectively.

The potentials of VB and CB of a semiconductor can be calculated according to the following empirical equations:
(2)
E VB
= X –
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
where E VB is the valence band edge potential, E CB is the conduction band edge potential, X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, which is the geometric mean of the electronegativity of the constituent atoms, E
e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5eV), E
g is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. Based on the Eqs. (2) and (3), the E CB potentials of g-C3N4 and AgVO3 are calculated to be −1.135 and −0.39eV, respectively. The E VB potentials of g-C3N4 and AgVO3 are determined to be 1.595 and 1.61eV, respectively. The photocatalytic mechanism for the g-C3N4/AgVO3 is thus proposed and schematically exhibited in Fig. S4c.

Under visible-light irradiation, both of g-C3N4 and AgVO3 absorb visible light and can be excited to produce h+ and e−. The electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 can easily migrate to that of AgVO3 because the CB potential of g-C3N4 is more negative than that of AgVO3, and electron transfer in this process is faster than the electron–hole recombination between the VB and CB of g-C3N4. In addition, because VB of g-C3N4 is above the VB of AgVO3, the holes in the VB of AgVO3 can also flow effectively into the VB of g-C3N4, and this process is also faster than the electron–hole recombination between the VB and CB of AgVO3. The heterostructure formed between g-C3N4 and AgVO3 prevents electrons and holes from recombination, which is beneficial for the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 3a shows the photocatalytic degradation curves of Rh B as a function of time. For the pure AgVO3 and g-C3N4, the concentration of Rh B was only reduced by about 61.9 and 81.9% after 40min of visible-light irradiation. Compared with the single-component a samples, the heterostructured g-C3N4 (1, 2, and 4%)/AgVO3 samples exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performances, which the concentration of Rh B is reduced by about 90.7, 100 and 88.4%, respectively. It should be noted that g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 sample showed higher photocatalytic efficiency than the sum of 6mg g-C3N4 and AgVO3 samples (82.5%). The reason is that efficient heterojunction interface between two components can restrain the recombination of photoinduced charges effectively [25–28]. The g-C3N4 coating can improve the visible light absorption efficiency (Fig. S4a), which is also beneficial for the composite to photolyze Rh B. The comparison of PL spectra of the pure AgVO3 and g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 under the excitation wavelength of 325nm is shown in Fig. 3b. In comparison with pure AgVO3, the intensity of the peak for g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 is much lower, which reveals that the composite sample has a lower recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. For evaluating the recyclability of the g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 sample, the additional experiments have been carried out to degrade Rh B under visible light cycled for four times. After reusing four cycles, the photodecomposition rate of Rh B still remained over 90% (Fig. 3c). In addition, there is no obvious difference in SEM images and XRD patterns (Fig. S5) of g-C3N4 (2%)/AgVO3 photocatalyst after the cycle measurement. The above result demonstrates that the g-C3N4/AgVO3 sample described in this paper has high stability in the photocatalytic process under visible-light irradiation.

High-efficiency visible-light-driven g-C3N4/AgVO3 photocatalyst was successfully synthesized. The as-prepared g-C3N4/AgVO3 nanowires exhibited excellent photocatalytic efficiency on the decolorization of Rh B, which was superior to those of pure g-C3N4 and AgVO3. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of the g-C3N4/AgVO3 may originate from the efficient separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs through the heterostructure composed of g-C3N4 and AgVO3.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21407059, 21576112, 51404108, 61308095), the Science Development Project of Jiangsu Province (BK20140527), and Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province (2015220).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.catcom.2016.10.021.
